run_summary: Screen 1 is positioned defensively this run: four open positions at day 14-15 (holding-window boundary) are being held or exited per time discipline, while six fresh flags from three distinct catalysts are evaluated. Four of the six flags (ONTO, PLAB, AEIS from Claude Tag; DOCN from Jalapeno) present conviction-tier conviction strength (threat_assessment='none', high confidence, crisp filings evidence), but they cluster into two catalyst-driven baskets—the basket rule applies, forcing them to exploratory tier to avoid concentration. PHR and CGNX remain exploratory on their lower threat or confidence.

entries.name: Onto Innovation Inc.
entries.catalyst: Anthropic's Claude Tag brings always-on AI teammate to Slack
entries.thesis: ONTO sells process-control equipment to advanced-node fabs (including AI accelerator manufacturing). A Slack AI integration has zero bearing on wafer inspection demand; if anything, more AI infrastructure buildout means more advanced-node capacity and more demand for ONTO's tools. The sharp drop is pure semi-cap sympathy on an unrelated AI headline.
entries.what_kills: Evidence of AI capex digestion at leading-edge fabs; customer concentration risk materializing (a key customer cutting orders); Rigaku regulatory snag.
entries.reasoning: ONTO passes BUY eligibility: OVERDONE, confidence 4 >= min 3, threat_assessment='none', panic_calibration='unjustified', not already held. Filings evidence is crisp (Risk Factors contain no AI-competition language; core business is metrology for advanced-node fabs, unaffected by Slack AI). However, ONTO is part of a three-name basket (ONTO, PLAB, AEIS) all triggered by the same 'Claude Tag' catalyst. Per basket rule, all three default to exploratory tier (6% per position) rather than conviction, to avoid 60%+ bankroll concentration on a single trigger reading. ONTO is already held as of portfolio snapshot, so this flag is redundant and should be logged as already-covered.

red_team.weakest_link: The bull case assumes Slack AI is truly unrelated to fab equipment demand, but if Claude Tag adoption accelerates enterprise software workloads that compete with on-premise AI chip demand, the knock-on effect on fab capex timing could materialize.
red_team.critique: The five-day decline of -5.98% is modest and within normal sympathy-fade range, but the real vulnerability is timing: the bull case asserts 'more AI infrastructure buildout means more demand' without addressing whether the current phase is digestion rather than expansion. The Slack integration specifically targets enterprise adoption of agentic workflows, which could depress near-term capex as enterprises optimize software-first before capital-heavy chip deployments. ONTO is already held, making this a redundant position rather than a fresh conviction.

entries.name: Photronics, Inc.
entries.catalyst: Anthropic's Claude Tag brings always-on AI teammate to Slack
entries.thesis: Photomasks are required for every semiconductor design release; AI chip proliferation increases design activity (Photronics explicitly notes demand depends on design activity, not just sales volume). A Slack AI integration has no mechanism to reduce photomask demand. The sharp drop is pure semi-sympathy noise.
entries.what_kills: A major customer (top 2 = 29% of revenue) cutting orders; consolidation in semiconductor customer base reducing design diversity.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: PLAB passes BUY eligibility: OVERDONE, confidence 4, threat_assessment='none', panic_calibration='unjustified', not already held. Filings evidence is strong (Risk Factors make no mention of AI threat; photomasks are physical inputs to every chip design; AI proliferation increases design activity). However, PLAB is part of the three-name Claude Tag basket (ONTO, PLAB, AEIS). Per basket rule, all three are exploratory tier (6% per position). PLAB is already held as of portfolio snapshot, so this flag is redundant and should be logged as already-covered.

red_team.weakest_link: Photomask demand is legitimately tied to design activity, but the thesis does not address whether design-cycle slowdowns precede capex cuts or whether recent design inflation is purely AI-accelerator-driven and thus vulnerable to margin compression.
red_team.critique: Volume multiple of 0.5 signals weak conviction-level selling, consistent with sympathy rather than fundamental repricing. However, the bull case glosses over customer concentration: if the top-2 customers (29% of revenue) are accelerator makers and the Slack news reduces their near-term SKU roadmaps, design activity could decelerate faster than historical cycle. PLAB is already held, redundancy applies here as well.

entries.name: Advanced Energy Industries, Inc
entries.catalyst: Anthropic's Claude Tag brings always-on AI teammate to Slack
entries.thesis: AEIS sells power conversion to both semiconductor fabs and AI data centers. The 10-K explicitly identifies AI as a demand driver they are scaling capacity to meet. A Slack AI integration is mechanically backwards as a bear catalyst — more enterprise AI deployment → more data-center power demand → more AEIS. The drop is pure sympathy.
entries.what_kills: Evidence of data-center buildout pause; semiconductor capex cycle peak signals.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: AEIS passes BUY eligibility: OVERDONE, confidence 4, threat_assessment='none', panic_calibration='unjustified', not already held. Filings evidence is the strongest of the basket—10-K explicitly names AI infrastructure investment as a demand tailwind driving power-conversion demand, and the company is scaling capacity to meet it. Slack AI integration is mechanically backwards as a bear. Despite threat_assessment='none' and conviction-strength evidence, AEIS is part of the three-name Claude Tag basket (ONTO, PLAB, AEIS). Per basket rule, all three default to exploratory tier (6% per position) to avoid disguising a single trigger bet as three independent convictions. AEIS is already held as of portfolio snapshot, so this flag is redundant and should be logged as already-covered.

red_team.weakest_link: The bull case correctly notes AEIS's 10-K flags AI as a tailwind, but does not quantify the mix between fab and data-center power demand or address whether data-center capex cycle inflection points are near.
red_team.critique: The five-day move of -1.54% is the smallest in the basket, and volume of 0.84x is tepid—classic sympathy fade noise. The real bear case would require evidence that data-center power conversions are already over-built relative to utilization, which is not presented. The thesis is strong and the sell-off is minor. AEIS is already held, making this redundant coverage.

entries.name: DigitalOcean Holdings, Inc.
entries.catalyst: OpenAI unveils its first custom chip, built by Broadcom
entries.thesis: OpenAI building its own inference chip is a vertical-integration story aimed at hyperscale AI inference economics — it has essentially zero direct read-through to DOCN's developer/SMB cloud business. DOCN is being lumped into 'cloud infrastructure providers at risk' by the trigger taxonomy, but its customer base and workload mix don't overlap with what Jalapeno disrupts. The sharp single-day drop on an already-extended week down looks like thematic AI-infra de-risking rather than a justified threat reaction.
entries.what_kills: Disclosure that a meaningful chunk of DOCN's AI/ML compute revenue comes from labs that might in-source (low probability given customer profile); guidance cut citing AI-infra competitive pricing; continued decline suggesting fundamental rather than sympathy selling.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: DOCN passes BUY eligibility: OVERDONE, confidence 3 (at min threshold), threat_assessment='minimal' (some non-zero risk, but filings evidence is clean), panic_calibration='unjustified'. Filings frame AI/ML as a growth vector; DOCN is SMB/developer IaaS, not hyperscale frontier-training. OpenAI custom-silicon story (Jalapeno/Broadcom) targets vertical integration at hyperscale inference economics—zero direct read-through to DOCN's customer base. Five-day move is -14.69% (extended), but single-day -5.47% on thematic de-risking rather than fundamental threat. Confidence is exactly at threshold (3), and threat_assessment='minimal', so exploratory tier (6% position) is appropriate. Sized as standalone flag, not part of a basket (only DOCN from Jalapeno catalyst; no other flags triggered by that catalyst today).

red_team.weakest_link: The bull case assumes Jalapeno (OpenAI's custom chip) has zero read-through to SMB/developer clouds, but if a credible inference-on-edge story emerges (bringing frontier model inference to cheaper tiers), DOCN's AI/ML revenue could face longer-term compression.
red_team.critique: The five-day decline of -14.69% is extended and suggests more than pure sympathy—this is a genuine crowd exit from cloud-infrastructure plays on Broadcom/OpenAI news. However, the timing and magnitude do not prove fundamental deterioration. DOCN's SMB customer base is structurally insulated from vertical integration at hyperscale inference economics. The single-day -5.47% move is material but consistent with thematic de-risking. Without a follow-up guidance cut or customer churn signal, this is a tradable fade rather than a broken thesis.

entries.name: Cognex Corporation
entries.catalyst: Anthropic's Claude Tag brings always-on AI teammate to Slack
entries.thesis: CGNX serves industrial QA on factory floors requiring deterministic latency and precision. A Slack AI teammate has zero overlap with industrial machine vision. While CGNX's filings do mention AI as a long-term competitive risk, today's selloff on an enterprise-collaboration news item is a sympathy fade, not a credible threat materializing.
entries.what_kills: Evidence of open-source vision platforms taking share at factory-floor accounts; weakness in the consumer electronics segment.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: CGNX passes BUY eligibility: OVERDONE, confidence 3 (at min threshold), threat_assessment='minimal', panic_calibration='unjustified'. Filings acknowledge AI as longer-term competitive consideration, but CGNX's products embed AI and serve deterministic factory-floor QA—not generic automation. Slack AI teammate has zero overlap with industrial machine vision. Filings evidence is thinner than the Claude Tag basket names (AI is flagged but not as immediate threat), and confidence is exactly at threshold. Exploratory tier (6% position) is appropriate. Standalone flag (only CGNX from this particular catalyst read; PHR is a different catalyst). CGNX is already held as of portfolio snapshot, so this flag is redundant and should be logged as already-covered.

red_team.weakest_link: The bull case asserts Slack AI has zero overlap with factory-floor QA, but if Slack adoption accelerates and enterprise AI adoption becomes visible-layer integrated (e.g., agents requesting QA data feeds), CGNX could face longer-term platform-risk pricing.
red_team.critique: The five-day move of -2.94% is minimal, and volume of 0.84x shows weak selling pressure. The thesis that industrial machine vision is deterministic and moat-protected is sound. Slack AI teammate is an enterprise-collaboration tool, not a computer-vision replacement. CGNX is already held, so this is redundant flag coverage.

entries.name: Phreesia, Inc.
entries.thesis: Agentic swarms target generic enterprise workflow automation; Phreesia's intake/engagement business is gated by HIPAA, EHR/PM integration plumbing, payer-specific eligibility, and life-sciences network contracts. A 'loopy' agent swarm cannot drop into a Cerner/Epic integration or take over patient-payment processing. The sympathy drop on a sub-average-volume day looks thematic, not fundamental.
entries.what_kills: An EHR vendor (Epic/Cerner) announcing native agentic intake; a Phreesia customer-cohort or net-retention deterioration in next print; a new risk-factor amendment naming agentic AI specifically.
entries.reasoning: Auto-converted: exploratory tier cap reached (6 simultaneous open). Original reasoning: PHR passes BUY eligibility: OVERDONE, confidence 3 (at min threshold), threat_assessment='minimal', panic_calibration='unjustified'. Filings mention AI as a general competitive concern, but core moat is HIPAA-gated EHR/PM integrations, payer-eligibility processing, and service contracts—not generic workflow automation. Agentic swarms cannot replicate that complexity. Sympathy drop on sub-average volume is consistent with the read. Filings evidence is moderate (AI risk flagged but moat mechanisms are specific and deep), and confidence is at threshold. Exploratory tier (6% position). Standalone flag. PHR is already held as of portfolio snapshot, so this flag is redundant and should be logged as already-covered.

red_team.weakest_link: The bull case dismisses agentic AI as unable to replicate HIPAA + EHR integration complexity, but if a major EHR vendor or payer network (United, CVS) announces native agentic intake, the moat narrative breaks and PHR's premium valuation becomes vulnerable.
red_team.critique: The five-day move of -1.55% and volume of 0.7x indicate light, sympathy-driven selling. The thesis that HIPAA and EHR/payer integration create structural moats is credible and matches the data: the sell-off is shallow. No follow-up healthcare-specific AI announcement has occurred, so the primary risk (EHR vendor competitive response) remains theoretical. PHR is already held, making this a redundant position.
